notes: NSCISC National SCI Database cohort 1995-2015. 80.8% of initial AIS-A complete injuries remained AIS-A at follow-up (30-730 days). 8.8% converted to motor incomplete (AIS C-E). Represents modern standard-of-care including spinal stabilization and vasopressors. Neurogenic shock typically accompanies high thoracic/cervical complete SCI. This is the most robust estimate for treated persistent paraplegia/tetraplegia among survivors.

European cohort assessed at 1 3 6 and 12 months. Approximately 70% of initial AIS-A patients did not convert at all during the first year. Provides a lower-bound validation estimate. n not extracted from abstract; full text required for exact denominator.

NCBI StatPearls 2025: only 19% of initial ASIA grade A patients improve by at least one AIS grade; 81% do not. Independent tertiary estimate aligning with Marino et al. No specific n reported in the review chapter.

No modern untreated cohort exists for ethical reasons. Historical pre-1938 combat TSCI shows only ~10% acute survival without modern care. Neurogenic shock without vasopressors has high acute mortality (StatPearls 2023). Among the rare survivors of untreated neurogenic shock persistent paraplegia/tetraplegia approaches universal because secondary injury mechanisms proceed unchecked and no recovery interventions are provided. Inferred from pathophysiology + historical mortality data.

Pre-1938 military TSCI: among the ~10% who survived the acute period without stabilization or vasopressors essentially all had permanent paraplegia/tetraplegia. No rehabilitation or stabilization was available. Historical observation not a controlled cohort.

If persistent impairment is broadened to include AIS B (sensory incomplete motor complete) and AIS C (motor incomplete with severe deficits) then 80.8% remaining AIS-A plus the 8.8% converting only to motor incomplete yields ~89.6% with significant persistent impairment. Adding estimated AIS-B patients gives ~92%. This captures paraplegia autonomic dysfunction and pressure-injury risk.
PTSD prevalence 15.9% in post-anaphylaxis Hymenoptera venom patients vs 2.4% local-reaction controls. Proxy for persistent psychiatric impairment at >=3 months in treated survivors.
12% of anaphylactic shock survivors fulfilled full PTSD diagnostic criteria. College-student cohort; treated population assumed.
Acute PTSD (IES-R-K >=25) in 41.4% of 203 anaphylaxis patients; 23.2% anxiety 28.1% depression. Acute assessment not confirmed >=3 months persistence.

Near-fatal anaphylaxis episodes can cause permanent neurological disability from hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy. Fraction represents near-fatal cases among all anaphylaxis; untreated progression to arrest drives this.
Among survivors of anaphylaxis-related out-of-hospital cardiac arrest only 24.5% had favorable neurological outcome at 1 month; 75.5% had unfavorable outcomes. Proxy for untreated severe progression.
Single pediatric case: memory deficits and confusion persisted at 1 year after severe anaphylaxis with hypoxic injury. Case report; not generalizable but confirms neurologic sequelae exist.

Mixed severe shock under aggressive ICU care. 36% disabled at mean 5.1yr. SF-36 PF 1.2 SD below norm. 53% return to work. Best proxy for treated traumatic hypovolemic shock. BROAD definition any persistent impairment.
Long-stay ICU patients at mean 2yr. 50% normal active lives; 44% disabled but independent; 6% needing daily support. SEVERE impairment only.
Major trauma ICU survivors at 6 months. 29% unable to return to work due to health. Functional incapacity proxy.

No direct long-term data. Based on 24% acute organ failure in day-1 survivors and historical military mortality differentials. Persistent impairment with minimal care estimated 0.45 range 0.35-0.55. BROAD definition.
Conservative lower-bound for SEVERE persistent impairment only. Aligns with acute organ failure rate plus TBI-associated deficits. Comparable to current placeholder.

2 of 19 treated survivors confirmed persistent airflow limitation at >=6 months (bronchiectasis/COPD pattern); 17 others not followed at referral hospital so 0.105 is a lower bound. Both cases remained impaired at 6-year follow-up.

All 3 patients evaluated 2 years after massive occupational exposure had persistent impairment (restrictive dysfunction obstructive disease bronchial hyper-reactivity). Selected severe-referral case series; high selection bias toward poor outcomes.
Single lifelong non-smoker followed 10 years after massive accidental exposure; severe fixed airways obstruction and mild bronchiectasis. Case report not generalizable.

Composite estimate: bronchopulmonary pathology 33-50% by 2002 (Bebeshko 2003); clinically significant cataracts in many survivors (UNSCEAR); skin scarring/ulceration a major cause of persistent disability (NEA 2002); CNS cardiovascular and endocrine late effects also increased. Union of organ-specific impairments exceeds 50%.
Upper-bound fraction with persistent bronchopulmonary pathology by 2002 in severe ARS survivors. Bebeshko 2003: increased from one-third to one-half of the survivors depending on the degree of ARS.

Clinically significant radiation cataracts detected in 11/75 survivors under long-term observation at Institute of Biophysics (Saratov 2015); threshold dose ~3.2 Gy. Number correlated directly with ARS severity (Bebeshko 2003). Many of 106 survivors developed cataracts in first few years (UNSCEAR).

Radiation skin burns observed in 56 patients (out of 237 initially diagnosed 134 confirmed ARS). NEA 2002: Cataracts scarring and ulceration are the most important causes of persistent disability in acute radiation sickness survivors. Beta burns were primary cause of death in several patients and caused persistent scarring/ulceration in survivors.

4 survivors from 8 ARS patients (4 died). All survivors had severe persistent radiation burns; one required arm amputation. n too small for generalization but shows 100% impairment in this small treated cohort.

No large untreated ARS survivor cohort exists because LD50/60 without supportive care is ~3.5-4.0 Gy (Mettler 2007). Untreated survivors limited to grades 1-2 (~1-3 Gy). Persistent impairment includes cataracts (OR 1.39 at 1 Gy RERF) pulmonary and cardiovascular late effects but composite lower than treated cohort because severe cases die without treatment. Conservative estimate based on A-bomb proxy data and Chernobyl mild-ARS trajectory.

Cataract surgery prevalence OR 1.39 at 1 Gy (95% CI 1.24-1.55); 20-30% excess cataracts at 1 Gy in A-bomb survivors. For ARS-relevant doses (2-5 Gy) extrapolated surgery-prevalence fraction ~0.35-0.60. Baseline unexposed prevalence ~10-15% by age 70. Threshold for visual-impairment cataract may be as low as 0.5 Gy.
Post-sepsis cognitive impairment in elderly Medicare beneficiaries at 1 year. Proxy for treated persistent impairment.
Severe disability (ADL/IADL limitations) in sepsis survivors at 1 year. Upper bound for treated persistent impairment.
Young healthy workforce (Danish military) 2.7% disability pension after sepsis. Lower bound for treated in analog-selectee population.

All-comers ED FBAO cohort n=407; 48.2% 1-month survival; 23.8% favorable neurologic outcome (CPC 1-2); unfavorable among survivors = 99/196 = 50.5%; elderly Japanese cohort median age 82; 1-month outcome used as proxy for >=3-month persistence because neurologic recovery after hypoxic injury plateaus by 30 days

In-hospital food FBAO n=300; 31% died; 26.7% residual disability; among 207 survivors 80 had residual disability = 38.7%; immediate suction/back blows/abdominal thrusts available; closest proxy for analog mission with trained CMO present; outcome at discharge used as proxy for persistent impairment
Small airways obstruction (elevated RV and reduced V25) present in 11/14 survivors at 6 months. 10/14 had concomitant skin burns. Treated in hospital with supplemental O2 and supportive care.
Of 7 patients reassessed at 2 years, all retained small airways obstruction. Subset of the 6-month cohort above. 10/14 original group had burns.

23 of 45 survivors assessed at ~45 months. Only 4/23 reported increased dyspnoea; spirometry and bronchial responsiveness were within normal limits for all. Combined smoke inhalation + body surface burns.

Retrospective case-control of burn-unit patients. INHI group (n=33) had 21% outpatient pulmonary sequelae (ineffective airway clearance, infections, SOB) vs 4% in non-ventilated controls. Combined burn+smoke.

Proxy for untreated smoke-inhalation CNS impairment. CO and HCN are key smoke components. Without supplemental O2 or hyperbaric therapy, 15-50% of survivors develop persistent/delayed neuropsychiatric sequelae (memory, cognition, parkinsonism). Midpoint 33% used. No direct untreated smoke-inhalation cohorts exist because treatment is standard of care.

Severe smoke-inhalation poisoning: 40% long-term neurological impairment including cognitive dysfunction, short-term memory deficits, and sensory-motor problems. Applies to severe exposures regardless of treatment access. Professional/trade source; used as bounding estimate.

National unselected EMS-treated OHCA cohort (N=147405). Survival to discharge 9.4%; CPC 1-2 7.5%. Impairment fraction = (13794-11110)/13794 = 0.195. Outcome measured at hospital discharge (~days to weeks).
Witnessed OHCA cohort (N=194807) from CARES 2013-2023. Survival to discharge 15.3%; favorable neurologic survival 12.9%. Impairment fraction among survivors = 0.157. Discharge outcome.

CARES 2013-2020 cohort (N=420671). Subgroup WITH bystander CPR (n~165702). Survival to discharge 13.1%; favorable neurologic survival 11.4%. Impairment fraction among survivors = 0.130. Discharge outcome.

Contemporary European ICU cohort (N=288 admitted to ICCU). Back-calculated: 93% of discharged patients had favorable neurologic outcome at 6 months (CPC 1-2). Survival to discharge ~52.8%. Impairment fraction among survivors = 0.072. Meets >=3-month persistence criterion.

Hypothermia Registry (N=986 TH-treated OHCA). ~50% survived; >90% good neurologic function at 6-12 months (CPC 1-2). Conservative impairment fraction estimated as 0.07. Selected ICU cohort with full post-arrest care (TTM, angiography, PCI). Meets >=3-month criterion.

Prospective ICU cohort (N=415 admitted). 2-year mortality 58.1%; 96.7% of survivors had good neurologic outcome at 2 years (CPC 1-2). Impairment fraction = 0.033. Long-term follow-up meets >=3-month criterion.

Same CARES cohort as above. Subgroup WITHOUT bystander CPR (n~254969) — best available proxy for untreated remote analog-mission scenario. Survival to discharge 7.3%; favorable neurologic survival 5.6%. Impairment fraction among survivors = 0.233. Patients still received EMS/ALS; true untreated OHCA is almost universally fatal.
SITS International 2021 IVT registry 2014-2021. mRS 0-1=42.4%; mRS 0-2=56.7%; death=16.3%. Among survivors mRS>=2=(14.3%+27.0%)/83.7%=49.3%. Ischemic stroke only; large multinational cohort.
Mixed MT+IVT vs IVT cohort n=640. mRS 0-1=47%; mRS 0-2=62%; mortality=13%. mRS>=2 among survivors=(96+161)/560=45.9%. Confirms registry estimate. Ischemic stroke only.
EVT-treated LVO cohort n=181 (median NIHSS 17-18). mRS 0-2=44.8%; mortality=20.4%. mRS>=2 among survivors=92/144=63.9%. Severe stroke treated proxy. Ischemic stroke only.
NINDS rt-PA Part 2 placebo arm n=165. mRS 0-1=26% (43/165); mortality=21% (~35/165). mRS>=2 among survivors=87/130=66.9%. Best available untreated all-comer ischemic stroke proxy.

Mild ischemic stroke (NIHSS<=5) without thrombolysis n=136. mRS 2-6=29% (40/136); deaths=3.7% (5/136). mRS>=2 among survivors=35/131=26.7%. Lower bound for untreated mild stroke; not generalizable to all stroke.

Oxford Vascular Study calibration targets (pre-IVT era; population-based). At 3 months: independence mRS 0-2=30.3%; disability mRS 3-5=47.5%; death=22.2%. mRS>=3 among survivors=47.5/77.8=61%. Note: mRS 2 counted as independence; true mRS>=2 is higher. Includes ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke.
Systematic review of 26 studies; ~28% of previously-employed burn survivors never return to any form of employment. Proxy for persistent functional impairment in modern treated cohorts.
Survey-based analysis of treated burn survivors; one-third self-identified as disabled due to burn injuries at long-term follow-up.
Burn Model System database; >25% report persistent physical symptoms (paresthesia, joint pain, temperature intolerance, balance trouble, memory difficulty) at 6-12-24 months post-injury.
Developing-world teaching-hospital cohort (Ghana); 42.9% had scar contractures, 35.7% disfigurements, 7.1% permanent disabilities (amputations). Higher than modern Western benchmarks.

Low-income country prospective cohort; 36% developed contracture limiting joint function at 12 months. Patients had delayed/suboptimal access to excision and grafting — closest available proxy for untreated persistent impairment.

No formal primary burn care; >80% had significant BSHS deficits (skin sensitivity, body image) at mean 4.7 years post-injury. SELECTION BIAS: cohort drawn from surgical-mission patients presenting for reconstruction; overestimates impairment among all untreated survivors. Only modern cohort explicitly without formal burn care.

Population-based retrospective cohort Canada 2009-2019. 42.0% of 6828 survivors to discharge required increased support in care from preadmission baseline (home care admission LTCH placement or increased caregiver hours). 47.5% readmitted within 1 year. Best single estimate for any persistent functional impairment.
Prospective single-center cohort. 53% of CS survivors with no baseline cognitive impairment had persistent cognitive impairment at 3-month follow-up (bMoCA). Cognitive domain only.
Nationwide Danish cohort 2005-2017. Among 2865 hospital survivors 21% (95% CI 19-22%) were hospitalized for heart failure within 1 year. 5-year HF hospitalization 40%.

SHOCK trial RCT. 87% of one-year survivors were NYHA I-II therefore 13% NYHA III-IV. n=302 randomized approximately 140 one-year survivors combined ERV and IMS arms. ERV was the only independent predictor of better functional outcome.

Single-center retrospective n=81. All patients had IABP 49% thrombolytics NO primary PCI. 37 in-hospital survivors (45.7%). Heart failure developed in 51.6% of discharged patients over mean 85-month follow-up. Best available proxy for limited/no reperfusion era.

SHOCK trial IMS arm n=150. IABP plus thrombolysis NO early revascularization. 34% of 2-week post-discharge survivors worsened or died between 2 weeks and 1 year vs 15% in ERV arm. 6-month mortality 63.1%. Only 44% remained stable functional trajectory vs 71% with ERV. Trajectory signal not a clean impairment fraction.

Pre-reperfusion era pre-1980s before thrombolytics IABP or PCI. In-hospital mortality 80-90% leaving only 10-20% survivors. No long-term functional cohorts exist. Inferred from natural history of large transmural infarct without reperfusion plus worse outcomes in IMS arm vs ERV and Drakos limited-intervention cohort. Estimated 60-80% of rare survivors develop severe chronic HF cognitive impairment or end-organ damage. FLAGGED as inferred due to absence of direct evidence.
13% developed chronic pancreatitis at 5 years after index acute pancreatitis episode. 96 survivors from initial n=122. Treated with standard medical care.
17% developed new-onset diabetes mellitus at 5 years after index acute pancreatitis episode. 96 survivors from initial n=122. Treated with standard medical care.

Pooled prevalence of pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (PEI) 27.1% (95% CI 20.3-35.1%) at mean 36 months follow-up after acute pancreatitis. Meta-analysis of 32 studies. Higher in severe (33.4%) vs mild (19.4%) AP.

Pooled prevalence of exocrine pancreatic insufficiency during follow-up (>=1 month) 35% (95% CI 27-43%) after acute pancreatitis. Meta-analysis of 39 follow-up studies. Decreased from 62% during admission.

Pooled prevalence of pancreatic exocrine insufficiency after acute pancreatitis 29% (95% CI 19-39%). Meta-analysis of 8 clinical studies. Concomitant PEI in 40% of those with new prediabetes/diabetes after AP.
Meta-analysis of 64 studies. PEI occurred in 22% overall (CI 17-27%) stabilizing at 26% (CI 17-39%) after the 1st year. Severe PEI 21% overall. Mild AP 10% severe PEI. Alcoholic etiology higher risk.

21 of 63 recreational divers (33%) with spinal cord decompression sickness had incomplete resolution after 1 month despite recompression treatment. Clinical severity at presentation was the only independent predictor of poor outcome (OR 2.68). Median treatment delay 3 hours did not influence recovery.

12 of 31 divers (38.7%) admitted with spinal cord decompression sickness had persistent neurological sequelae. 19 fully recovered. Both casual and professional divers included. Treated with standard recompression.

19.7% of 208 retired Norwegian offshore divers reported neurological DCS episodes. Divers with neurological DCS had significantly more neurological findings on motility sensory and coordination tests vs no-DCS divers. Long-term effects independent of acute symptomatology. Treated population.

7 astronauts after long-duration spaceflight: optic disc edema 5/7 globe flattening 5/7 choroidal folds 5/7 cotton-wool spots 3/7 RNFL thickening 6/7 decreased near vision 6/7. All had persistent structural changes post-flight. Treated NASA astronauts with full medical surveillance.

Prospective cohort of 11 astronauts on 6-month ISS missions. Axial length decrease of 0.08mm persisted through last examination at 1 year post-flight (P<.001). Peripapillary choroidal thickening and optic disc edema observed bilaterally. All treated with NASA standard care.

3 astronauts with posterior globe flattening persisting for 7 years or more after long-duration spaceflight suggesting permanent scleral remodelling. All were treated NASA astronauts. Denominator is the described persistent cohort only.

Approximately 60% of astronauts on long-duration missions and 29% on short-duration missions experienced degradation in distant and near visual acuity per postflight questionnaires (n~300). Some vision changes remain unresolved for years after flight. Treated population with NASA medical surveillance.

Approximately 1 in 3 astronauts flying long-duration ISS missions have presented with >=1 ocular findings (optic disc edema hyperopic shifts globe flattening choroidal/retinal folds cotton wool spots). Review article citing NASA operational data. Treated population. Denominator not explicitly stated in abstract.

Major trauma cohort (n=433) 1-year follow-up. 40-70% specific limitations; 60% reported functional limitations on 2+ EQ-5D domains. 18% had at least one functional limitation. NOT abdominal-specific: mixed trauma cohort including TBI, extremity, spine, and abdominal injuries. Used as best available proxy because no dedicated abdominal-trauma impairment cohorts with exact fractions were found. Abdominal injury was present in 31% of Evans 2003 major-trauma survivors but persistence not isolated.

Severe abdominal trauma PROs study (Damage Control Laparotomy trial). Functional deficits persist beyond acute setting regardless of laparotomy status. PROs remain persistently poor at 6 months. Exact impairment fraction not reported in abstract; 0.55 is an inferred midpoint based on persistently poor EQ-5D and SF-36 trajectories. No denominator extractable from available sources. Limited abdominal-trauma-specific long-term cohort data.

No modern untreated abdominal-trauma cohorts exist for ethical reasons. Historical military and pre-modern surgical data suggest that untreated penetrating abdominal trauma had very high mortality (peritonitis, sepsis). Among rare survivors without surgical repair, persistent impairment from adhesions, bowel obstruction, and chronic pain approaches universal. Inferred from pathophysiology and historical mortality differentials. FLAGGED as inferred due to absence of direct evidence.

Tibial fracture with acute compartment syndrome. On lower extremity functional scale at 1 year: 48% severely disabled, 35% moderately disabled, 17% minimal disability. n=32 tibial fractures with ACS evaluated. Treated with fasciotomy and fracture fixation. Mean compartment pressure correlated with disability severity. Direct cohort evidence for treated ACS.

No modern untreated ACS cohorts exist for ethical reasons. Historical pre-fasciotomy era data and pathophysiology indicate that untreated ACS progresses to Volkmann ischemic contracture with near-universal severe disability (muscle necrosis, nerve damage, contracture). Fasciotomy within 6 hours is the standard of care; delays markedly increase amputation and long-term disability risk. Inferred from historical case series and pathophysiology. FLAGGED as inferred.

Workers compensation lumbar fusion cohort. Permanent disability rate 11% for surgical cases vs 2% for nonoperative controls at 2 years. n=725 cases vs 725 controls. 36% had complications. 76% continued opioid use after surgery. Specific to compensated lumbar fusion population; may underestimate broader back-injury impairment because surgical candidates are selected for severity.

Major trauma cohort 1-year follow-up. Back/spine injury was identified as a prognostic factor for long-term disability. 40-70% specific limitations; 60% on 2+ EQ-5D domains. Used as proxy for traumatic back injury because dedicated back-injury impairment cohorts with exact fractions are sparse. Represents mixed-severity treated trauma population.

Prospective population-based study of workers with back injuries (N=1883) interviewed at 18 days and 1 year. 42% received opioids; 16% were long-term users (4 quarters). Among long-term users, only 26% showed clinically important improvement in pain and 16% in function. 15% is a lower-bound estimate for persistent work-disabling back injury in a treated workers-comp population.

PariS-TBI severe TBI inception cohort. Among 257 survivors, 134 evaluated at 1 year: 38% severe disability, 43% moderate disability, 19% good recovery. Impairment fraction = 81% with persistent disability (moderate + severe). Prospective observational study in Parisian area. Treated with modern neurosurgery/ICU care.

PariS-TBI 4-year follow-up. Among 245 survivors, 147 evaluated: 32% severe disability, 40% moderate disability, 28% good recovery. Impairment fraction = 72% with persistent disability. Confirms 1-year estimate with some recovery over time but most persistent impairment remains. Meets >=3-month criterion.

Traumatic Brain Injury Model Systems (TBIMS) long-term data cited in PMC10008475. At 5 years after TBI, 57% of survivors were moderately or severely disabled. 55% previously employed were unemployed; 33% dependent for ADLs. Represents modern treated severe TBI population across US trauma centers. Independent validation of PariS-TBI estimates.

No modern untreated severe TBI cohorts exist for ethical reasons. Pre-modern neurosurgery era (pre-1970s) severe TBI mortality was 50-70% in first weeks. Among rare survivors without modern ICU/neurosurgery, persistent severe disability (vegetative state, severe cognitive impairment, motor deficits) approaches universal. Inferred from historical mortality data and natural history. FLAGGED as inferred.

Baltimore hip fracture cohort (n=594) compared to community-dwelling aged (EPESE). At 12 and 24 months, about 50% of hip fracture patients were walking disabled vs 21-29% of community controls after adjustment for age, sex, comorbidities, and pre-fracture function. Treated with surgical repair. Direct cohort evidence for treated hip fracture persistent mobility impairment.

Systematic review of long-term disability after hip fracture. Approximately 60-75% independent in basic ADLs pre-fracture decreases to 40-60% post-fracture. 10-20% of survivors institutionalized. 26 per 100 disabled for walking 3m at 2 years. Represents modern treated cohorts across multiple countries. Composite estimate for any persistent functional impairment.

Systematic review of non-operative hip fracture management. Non-operative management is associated with high risk of hip displacement, increased pain, and loss of mobility. 1-year mortality significantly higher than operative cohort. Among survivors of non-operative management, near-universal severe persistent impairment due to pain, immobility, and complications. Exact fraction not quantified in review; 0.85 is inferred from clinical description of outcomes. FLAGGED as inferred proxy.

Traumatic thoracic and lumbar spine fractures (n=80) at 2-7 years follow-up. 76% reported no pain; 24% had persistent pain. Most patients able to return to work with comparable QoL to general population. Represents neurologically intact or minimally impaired treated cohort. Neurologic deficits in 25-60% depending on fracture classification (PMC8990395), which would raise impairment fraction for more severe fractures.

Clinical long-term consequences of thoracolumbar fracture and osteosynthesis. At follow-up, mean Eifel score significantly higher (worse) in patient cohort vs controls (4.7 vs 2.6, p=0.008). Dallas score superior for daily activities, work-leisure, and sociability. Chronic back pain and long sick leaves common. Very long-term postoperative outcome more difficult than expected in majority. 35% inferred persistent impairment from worse functional scores and chronic pain.

Retrospective cohort of burst-type thoracolumbar fractures in Brazil. Neurologic deficits present in 25-60% of lumbar fractures depending on classification. For operated burst fractures with some neurologic involvement, persistent impairment from pain, neurologic deficit, and kyphosis is common. 0.40 is a mid-range estimate for treated thoracolumbar burst fractures with variable neurologic involvement. Lower bound for stable fractures without neurologic deficit is ~0.24.

No modern untreated lumbar fracture cohorts exist. Historical and pre-surgical-era data indicate that unstable lumbar fractures without stabilization had high rates of chronic pain, progressive deformity, and neurologic deterioration. Among survivors without operative care, persistent impairment from kyphosis, chronic pain, and potential cauda equina syndrome is very common. Inferred from pathophysiology and pre-modern surgical literature. FLAGGED as inferred.

Trauma center cohort of patients with delayed diagnosis of cervical spine injuries. Of 50 survivors (81.9% of initial 61), neurologic deficits were present in 15 (30%), including 8 with complete spinal cord injuries. Represents treated trauma-center population where injuries were missed initially but eventually received care. Direct cohort evidence for persistent neurologic impairment among survivors.

Pediatric cervical spine injury cohort (n=258). Poor neurologic outcomes: death 30%, persistent neurologic deficit 43% among those with poor outcomes. Selection bias toward more severe injuries. 43% represents upper-bound estimate for persistent neurologic deficit in a treated pediatric trauma population. Adult treated rates likely lower (closer to 30% from Schwartz 1991).

Adult traumatic cervical spinal cord injury cohort (n=54 retrospective). 30% AIS A, 7% AIS B, 41% AIS C, 22% AIS D. Among complete/incomplete SCI survivors, persistent impairment is very high. For general neck injury (including fractures without SCI), persistent impairment is lower. 0.30 is a blended estimate for treated adult cervical trauma survivors with mixed neurologic involvement. Derived from multiple cervical spine cohorts.

No modern untreated cervical spine injury cohorts exist. Historical pre-stabilization era data and pathophysiology indicate that unstable cervical fractures without immobilization or surgery had high rates of progressive neurologic deterioration, chronic pain, and deformity. Among survivors without operative care, persistent impairment approaches universal for unstable injuries and is very high even for stable fractures due to chronic pain and limited mobility. Inferred from historical surgical literature and pathophysiology. FLAGGED as inferred.

KORINNA cohort n=333 elderly AMI survivors. 52.9% disabled at hospital discharge (HAQ-DI >0.49 minor to severe disability). Younger subgroup (65-75) had 36.8% disability. Treated with standard-of-care including reperfusion where indicated.

Health and Retirement Study population-based cohort. Most MI hospitalizations associated with significant increases in functional disability at time of event and in decade afterward. Survivors gained ~1.5-3.5 new functional limitations over 10 years. Broad estimate for any persistent impairment in treated MI survivors.

Pre-thrombolysis/pre-PCI era (before 1980s). No primary PCI or thrombolytics. Inferred from natural history of transmural infarct without reperfusion: higher rates of chronic heart failure reduced ejection fraction and functional limitation. KORINNA showed reperfusion strongly reduced disability. Estimated 60-80% of survivors had persistent functional limitation. FLAGGED as inferred.

NHATS nationally representative cohort of older adults with AF. After 7 years one-third had ADL disability independent of stroke. One-half had IADL disability. One-tenth in nursing home. AF accelerates physical function decline over 15-year follow-up (Europace).
Same NHATS cohort. 50% IADL disability at 7 years. Broader functional impairment measure in treated AF patients receiving standard rate/rhythm control and anticoagulation.

No modern untreated AF cohort exists because treatment is standard of care. Inferred from: (1) untreated AF has 5x stroke risk and AF-associated strokes are more severe and disabling; (2) progressive tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy and heart failure without rate/rhythm control; (3) Europace 15-year follow-up showed AF independently accelerates functional decline. Estimated persistent impairment 50-60% at >=3 years. FLAGGED as inferred.

First-episode psychosis cohort n=142. At 10-year follow-up only 32.4% met criteria for adequate functioning (employment plus social relationships); 67.6% had persistent functional impairment. Proxy for treated acute behavioral emergency with psychotic features.

Systematic review and meta-analysis of FEP 3-year outcomes. Mean symptomatic remission rate 36% (range 17-78%). Full recovery (symptomatic plus functional) lower at ~38%. Therefore ~62% persistent impairment. Broader treated proxy for acute behavioral emergency.

No direct cohort for untreated acute behavioral emergency exists. Inferred from: (1) 70% of suicide attempters discharged from ED never attend first outpatient appointment (SPRC); (2) FEP with longer duration of untreated psychosis (DUP) has worse functional outcomes; (3) persistent mood symptoms strongly predict functional impairment in bipolar disorder. Estimated 75-85% persistent functional impairment without treatment. FLAGGED as inferred.

470 previously untreated patients with newly diagnosed epilepsy. 47% became seizure-free on first AED and 14% on second or third drug. 39% never achieved seizure freedom despite multiple drug trials. Persistent seizures represent persistent impairment. Treated cohort with modern AEDs.

NHANES nationally representative cross-sectional study. 80% of patients treated for seizures or epilepsy reported at least 1 disability in physical cognitive or social functioning vs 38% without epilepsy. Broader definition of persistent impairment in treated epilepsy.

In low-income countries 75% of people with epilepsy do not receive treatment (WHO). Untreated persistent seizures lead to cumulative brain injury cognitive decline psychosocial disability and increased mortality. Treated cohorts already show 39-80% persistent impairment; untreated estimated higher at 80-90% due to uncontrolled seizures and lack of neuroprotection. Conservative estimate 0.85. FLAGGED as inferred.

Prospective cohort of young patients (15-35 years) with primary traumatic anterior shoulder dislocation treated nonoperatively. On survival analysis instability developed in 55.7% within 2 years and 66.8% by 5 years. 86.7% of recurrent instability occurred within first 2 years. Recurrent instability is a strong proxy for persistent functional impairment in this population.

Review of literature on recurrent anterior shoulder instability. After open surgical stabilization recurrent instability rates ~13% in general populations lower than arthroscopic (~25% in high-risk athletes). 0.13 represents best-case treated lower bound for persistent functional impairment after definitive surgical repair.

Systematic review of chronic anterior shoulder dislocation outcomes. Most studies report poor Rowe scores compared to surgically treated counterparts. High rates of persistent pain and dysfunction with nonsurgical treatment of chronic dislocations. Sahu systematic review confirmed similarly poor outcomes. Proxy for untreated shoulder dislocation without reduction or stabilization.

Retrospective observational study of 48 eyes in White Caucasian APAC patients. Mean FU 27 months. 6% blind + 10% visually impaired per WHO criteria at final visit. Treated with LPI. Low long-term visual morbidity in this population.

Retrospective case series of 121 eyes (117 patients) with APAC at Moorfields Eye Hospital UK. 15% had severe visual impairment (blindness per WHO) in affected eye at final follow-up. 73% achieved 6/12 or better. Treated predominantly with LPI. Delayed presentation linked to worse outcomes.

Prospective cohort of 50 fungal corneal ulcers from Aravind Eye Hospital India at 4-year FU. 32% had BSCVA worse than 20/60 (moderate visual impairment). 24% had BSCVA worse than 20/400 (severe impairment). Treated with antifungals. Same study as bacterial subgroup below.

Prospective cohort of 50 bacterial corneal ulcers from Aravind Eye Hospital India at 4-year FU. 24% had BSCVA worse than 20/60. 8% had BSCVA worse than 20/400. Treated with antibiotics. Same study as fungal subgroup above.

Retrospective cohort of 314 open globe injury eyes in Tianjin China. Mean FU 8.4 months. 61.1% had final VA worse than 20/200 (legal blindness). Treated surgically. Includes rupture laceration and penetrating injuries. Independent predictors: initial VA RAPD zone of injury.

Retrospective cohort of 311 open globe injury patients in Thailand 2016-2023. 42.77% had VA worse than 20/200 at 6-month follow-up. Median FU 4.14 months. Modern vitrectomy era. Treated surgically. Revised Ocular Trauma Score C-statistic 0.8671.

Retrospective cohort of 356 SSNHL patients in China. 36.24% non-recovery (persistent hearing loss) after treatment. Mean age 45.8 years. Treated with systemic steroids and/or intratympanic injection. Nomogram ROC AUC 0.867.

Prospective cohort of 130 idiopathic SSNHL patients in Italy. 56.1% attained incomplete recovery at 2-month re-evaluation after treatment. All received systemic steroids. Incomplete recovery defined as persistent hearing threshold elevation. 17 patients showed delayed recovery at later follow-up.

Large prospective UK cohort of 2074 successfully re-attached macula-off RD eyes. 51.2% had persistent visual impairment (post-op VA >0.30 LogMAR i.e. worse than 6/12) at median follow-up. Treated with vitrectomy and internal tamponade. Key modifiable risk factor: duration of central vision loss.

Retrospective case-note review of 185 macula-off RD patients in a UK vitreoretinal unit. 44% regained 6/12 or better so 56% had persistent visual impairment (worse than 6/12). Median improvement 0.78 logMAR. Treated surgically. Anatomical success 84%.
